claimText: FPIs are restricted to holding a maximum of 10 percent of an Indian company's total paid-up equity capital.
evidenceExcerpt: FPIs are restricted to holding a maximum of 10 percent of an Indian company's total paid-up equity capital.
examUse: Statement-based question on FPI investment limits.
distractorHooks: Limit applies to debt capital instead of equity capital

claimText: If an FPI exceeds the 10 percent equity cap, it must either divest the surplus shares or reclassify the investment as Foreign Direct Investment (FDI).
evidenceExcerpt: Upon exceeding the 10 percent cap, FPIs have the option to divest surplus shares or reclassify their investment as FDI.
examUse: Statement-based question on the regulatory consequences of breaching FPI limits.
distractorHooks: Reclassification to FDI is not allowed under any circumstance

claimText: The 2024 RBI and SEBI framework prohibits the reclassification of FPI to FDI in sectors where Foreign Direct Investment is prohibited.
evidenceExcerpt: The 2024 RBI and SEBI framework prohibits FPI to FDI reclassification in sectors where FDI is already prohibited.
examUse: Statement-based question on sector-specific restrictions for FPI reclassification.
distractorHooks: Reclassification is allowed in FDI-prohibited sectors with special RBI approval
FPIs can bypass FDI sectoral caps through reclassification

claimText: Reclassifying FPI to FDI requires government approval for investments from land-bordering countries and mandates the concurrence of the Indian Investee Company.
evidenceExcerpt: Reclassification requires government approval for investments from land-bordering countries and mandates the concurrence of the Indian Investee Company.
examUse: Statement-based question on approval mechanisms for foreign investments from bordering nations.
distractorHooks: Concurrence of the Indian Investee Company is not required
Government approval is required for all FPI reclassifications regardless of the country of origin
